96.16: (hrū) tampuhnya n paṅĕne wimāna gumuruh sākṣāt garantaṅ humuṅ (a clattering sound?).

under garantuṅ gives: ``alongside ... gĕṇḍiṅ paṅkon, a certain musical instrument'', suggesting that the garantuṅ is an instrument that hangs, in contrast to a gĕṇḍiṅ that lies on the lap. In Bali, acc. to Kunst-Kunst van Wely (p. 104 ff), the grantaṅ is an instrument having suspended cylindrical bamboo keys, similar to the gambaṅ. Acc. to GR, (udan) gĕrantaṅ is ``a sound in the air as of falling rain''; cf BK 96.16 above.

7.77a: paran ta kaṅ (read kakaṅ?) punika dereṅ tama iṅambil dera Pañji agĕgĕnḍiṅa#na. Gĕṇḍiṅ is frequently found in descriptions of armies on the march, the royal train, processions, dancing and celebrations, often in combination with goṅ, gubar, paḍahi, kĕṇḍaṅ, mṛdaṅga, bheri, but also alone.

29.8 shows that it was struck (tinabĕh). The fact that taṅguli gĕṇḍiṅ, t. garantaṅ and t. kĕtur appear to be interchangeable may suggest that gĕṇḍiṅ, garantaṅ (garantuṅ) and kĕtur were similar. Cf also manuk gĕṇḍiṅ, m. garantuṅ, m. titiran, m. kĕtur, and mṛdaṅga as suitable waṅsalan for gĕṇḍiṅ (NR 31.18).

definition: a musical instrument (used to accompany one's singing). The example from U.Pṅr. (see below under aṅgeroṅ) and GR (s.v. geroṅ): ``name of a melody; also a kind of oval waluh (gourd)'', suggest that the instrument possessed (or was constructed of) gourds; it was held on the lap (cf also the reference to breasts, Mal 9.128a below). For such an instrument see Kunst 1968, Fig. 64). Regarding the term geroṅan Kunst is right when he says: ``It refers to choral singing with gamĕlan accompaniment in the Javanese principalities''. Cf Horne, Javanese-English Dictionary: geroṅ, male chorus accompanying gamelan music; geroṅan, choral singing; ṅgeroṅi, to accompany an instrumental melody with unison singing. It is remarkable that this is not given by GR nor by Pig.
